coredata.dc:title: A novel preparation of water-dispersed graphene and their application to electrochemical detection of dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Jie
Wu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In the paper, water-dispersed graphene-GQDs composites were prepared by electrochemical exfoliation under alternating voltage combining ultrasonic treatment. The effects of alternating voltage, alternating frequency and the distance between electrodes were carefully explored. The results showed that the quality of composites prepared by alternating voltage was higher than that by direct voltage. The existence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hindered the agglomeration of graphene and facilitated dispersion of graphene in water. The sensor based on the obtained graphene-GQDs composites was used to detect dopamine (DA). The electrochemical investigation showed that the sensor had good selectivity and wide linear ranges for the detection of DA (0.1–100 µM). The detection limit could be down to 3 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3) with a sensitivity of 14.25 µA µM−1 cm−2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water-dispersed graphene
Electrochemical exfoliation
Alternating voltage
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphene using alternating voltage
Effect of the distance between electrodes
Dispersibility of samples prepared by alternating voltage and direct voltage
Electrochemical behavior of DA at graphene-GQDs modified electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of graphene-GQDs composites by the electrochemical exfoliation with alternating voltage and ultrasonic treatment.

(a) Line chart of current with different voltages, (b) histograms of the graphite quantity of exfoliation with different voltages, (c) histograms of concentration of graphene with different voltages under direct current voltage and alternating voltage (0.01 Hz and 0.1 Hz).
TEM images of graphene prepared at (a) ±5 V, (b) ±6 V, (c) ±8 V, (d) ±10 V. The alternating frequency is fixed at 0.01 Hz.
Raman spectra of graphene prepared at different alternating voltages (5 V, 6 V, 8 V and 10 V).

XRD spectra (a, b) of the graphite prepared at 0.01 Hz, 0.03 Hz, 0.05 Hz, 0.1 Hz, and direct voltage; FT-IR spectra (c) of the graphene prepared at 0.01 Hz, 0.1 Hz, and direct voltage; Raman spectra (d) of the graphene prepared at 0.01 Hz, 0.03 Hz, 0.05 Hz, 0.1 Hz, and direct voltage. The voltage and the distance between plates is fixed at ±8 V and 2 cm, respectively.
TEM images of graphene prepared at (a) 0.01 Hz, (b) 0.03 Hz, (c) 0.05 Hz, and (d) 0.1 Hz. The voltage and the distance between plates are fixed at ±8 V and 2 cm, respectively.
Raman spectra of the graphene prepared at the electrode distance of 1 cm, 2 cm, 3 cm, 4 cm, and 6 cm. The voltage and the alternating frequency are fixed at ±8 V and 0.01 Hz, respectively.
TEM images of graphene prepared at (a) 1 cm, (b) 2 cm, (c) 4 cm, and (d) 6 cm. The voltage and the alternating frequency are fixed at ±8 V and 0.01 Hz, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the electrochemical exfoliation with alternating voltage of graphite plate.
(a) EIS spectra and (b) CV curves (scan rate: 20 mV s−1) of bare GCE, GQDs, graphene, graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage and direct voltage in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6)]3−/4− (0.1 M KCl) solution.

(a) CV obtained at bare GCE, graphene, GQDs, graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage and direct voltage in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 mM DA at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1; (b) CV curves of the graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage 0.1 mM DA in different pH of 5.6, 6.0, 6.6, 7.0, 7.6 and 8 at scan rate of 10 mV s−1; (c) plot of peak potentials vs. pH; (d) CV response of different scan rate (10–100 mV s−1) at graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 6) containing 0.1 mM DA (top left corner: the plot of Ip vs square root of scan rates, top right corner: the plot of Ep vs logarithm of scan rates); (e) DPV response of graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 6) with different DA concentrations (0.1, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM); (f) The Plots of peak current versus the concentration of DA.
Electrochemical performances of dopamine sensors based on different sensing materials.

head.ce:title: A novel preparation of water-dispersed graphene and their application to electrochemical detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou Polytechnic Institute, Yangzhou 225000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou Polytechnic Institute, Yangzhou 225000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites and College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites and College of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites and College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the paper, water-dispersed graphene-GQDs composites were prepared by electrochemical exfoliation under alternating voltage combining ultrasonic treatment. The effects of alternating voltage, alternating frequency and the distance between electrodes were carefully explored. The results showed that the quality of composites prepared by alternating voltage was higher than that by direct voltage. The existence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hindered the agglomeration of graphene and facilitated dispersion of graphene in water. The sensor based on the obtained graphene-GQDs composites was used to detect dopamine (DA). The electrochemical investigation showed that the sensor had good selectivity and wide linear ranges for the detection of DA (0.1–100 µM). The detection limit could be down to 3 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3) with a sensitivity of 14.25 µA µM−1 cm−2.

ce:section.ce:para: Since its introduction in 2004, graphene has become a hot topic in business and academia due to its excellent properties as well as to its potential applications in numerous fields [1]. At present, the methods for preparing graphene are divided into two categories: 1) bottom-up, 2) top-down. The bottom-up methods include chemical vapor deposition [2] and epitaxial growth [3], which have rigorous requirements for operating conditions. The top-down methods include mechanical exfoliation [4], reduction of graphene oxide [5,6], liquid-shear-exfoliation [7,8], and intercalation and expansion of graphite [9]. In all these ways, the reduction of graphene oxide and liquid-shear-exfoliation are two the core routes for large-scale preparation of graphene. The chemical and thermal reduction of graphene oxide are cheap. However, the reduced graphene oxide still has many defects due to partial recovery of sp3 structure in reduction process, which hinders application of graphene in electronic field. In addition, liquid-shear-exfoliation in solvents can obtain high-quality graphene on a large scale. However, this method still exists some limitations in preparing graphene with less layers and small size.

Electrochemical exfoliation is facile, low cost, environmentally friendly, and controllable [10–14]. Electrochemical exfoliation can be divided into anodic and cathodic exfoliation. Anodic exfoliation is most common because it can easily produce large amount of thin graphene in a short time. The electrolytes of anodic exfoliation include ionic liquids [15] and aqueous solution [12,16]. The former usually results in a low yield of graphene with a small lateral size. At the same time, graphene is often functionalized with the ionic liquids, which disrupts the electronic properties of graphene [17]. The latter can produce large amount of graphene with a large lateral size. However, graphene prepared by this method contains a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups due to excessive oxidation of graphite [12,14,18]. In order to further improve the carbon–oxygen ratio of graphene, melamine [19] or antioxidants [20] have been introduced to prevent oxidation of graphene during anodic exfoliation. However, such approach has two disadvantages: the use of expensive additives and difficulty in removing adsorbed additives from the graphene. Moreover, the restacking of graphene in water is another fatal drawback, which can cause a severe decrease of the electrochemically accessible surface area. So the good dispersion and the low oxidation degree of graphene prepared by anodic exfoliation still have some challenges.

DA is a kind of brain secretion, which is the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain. It can regulate many physiological functions of the central nervous system [21,22]. Changes in the concentration of DA lead to neurological diseases [23–28]. Thus, sensitive and accurate method for detection of DA is highly essential. Due to simple operation, fast response, low-cost, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity, electrochemical method can be effectively used for detection of DA [29]. However, green, economic, highly sensitive and selective sensing materials still need to be developed.

In this work, alternating voltage was employed to exfoliate high purity graphite plates in inorganic sodium sulfate aqueous solution. Note that the positive voltage activates the exfoliation and oxidizes the graphene sheets. When the voltage is switched to negative voltage, the produced functional groups are reduced. The use of two graphite plates as electrodes contributes to the improvement of exfoliation rate, and the use of alternating voltage reduces the oxidation of graphite. In the preparation process, a large number of GQDs were found among graphene sheets, and their sizes were about 1–4 nm. The existence of GQDs helped to alleviate the agglomeration of graphene and facilitated the dispersion of graphene in water. In addition, the potential value of as-prepared materials in detection of DA was also explored. The experimental results showed that the sensor based on the graphene-GQDs composites possessed a good sensitivity and selectivity for electrochemical detection of DA.

High purity graphite plates (99.99%, 100 × 50 × 2 mm) were bought from Beijing Jinglong Te Carbon Graphite Plant. Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), ethanol (99.8%), isopropylalcohol (IPA), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) and Disodium hydrogenorthophosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Plant. Dopamine hydrochloride (98%), uric acid (UA, 99%), and ascorbic acid (AA, 99%) were from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene using alternating voltage
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite plates were employed as cathode and anode. Two electrodes were immersed in 1 M Na2SO4 solution (400 mL). Two graphite electrodes were placed parallel at a certain distance. Replacing direct voltage with alternating voltage during exfoliation, in which anodic working bias switched several seconds from positive voltage to negative voltage, and vice versa. During this process, gas bubbles formed in both electrodes. After electrochemical exfoliation, the exfoliated graphene was collected with a membrane filter and rinsed with water to remove residual salts. Subsequently, the wet exfoliated produces were sonicated in NMP, water, IPA, or ethanol for 2 h. Then the resulting dispersion was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to sediment insufficiently exfoliated materials. The schematic illustration was presented in Fig. 1 . The obtained supernatant was collected and stored for further use.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed with a CHI660E. The Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) was used as a reference electrode and Pt wire was used as a counter electrode. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with sensing materials was used as working electrode. The prepared materials were dispersed in water (1 mg mL−1), and 5 μL of sample was dropped on the surface of GCE. The CV curves were carried out in 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) containing 0.1 mM DA solution.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on Bruker D8A X-ray diffractometer from 5° to 60°. Raman spectra were performed on a Renishaw using a 514 nm laser beam. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was implemented with a Nicolet model 8700 spectrometer (Nicolet Instrument Corporation, USA). Morphological data of samples were obtained from transmission electron microscope (TEM, HT-7700) and high- resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, H-9500).

In this paper, the electrochemical exfoliation of graphite plate was performed in 1 M Na2SO4 solution using alternating voltage (generated by a homemade setup). The effects of different voltages, alternating frequency and the distance between electrodes on the products were studied.

Under certain the distance between electrodes (2 cm), alternating frequency (0.01 Hz, 0.1 Hz) and time (5 h), a series of experiments were conducted at different voltages (5, 6, 8, 10 V). In Fig. 1a, it is obvious that with the increase of voltage, the current increases gradually. At the same voltage, the current of direct voltage is larger than that of alternating voltage. It can also be seen from the Fig. 2 a that the magnitude of the current is only dependent on the voltage and independent on the alternating frequency. With the increase of voltage, the quantity of the exfoliated graphite increases gradually (Fig. 2b). At the same voltage, because direct voltage has a higher current value, it obtains the most exfoliated graphite. That is to say, the higher the current value is, the faster the graphite will fall off. Because the high alternating frequency in the same exfoliating time has more exfoliating opportunities, the quantity of exfoliated graphite in high alternating frequency is more. The same amount of graphite obtained by electrochemical exfoliation were dispersed in NMP and treated by ultrasound for 2 h. The obtained supernatant after centrifugation for 10 min at 5000 rpm for UV test. Fig. 2c shows the relationship between the supernatant concentration of graphene and the voltage. With the increase of voltage, the concentration of graphene increases first and then decreases. And it has a maximum concentration at 8 V. Those also show that graphite can’t be expanded and exfoliated effectively under high voltage and high current.

Fig. 3 shows the TEM images of graphene obtained at different voltages with an alternating frequency of 0.01 Hz. With the increase of voltage, the size of graphene sheet increases. And many small dots were observed. To further study, HR-TEM was employed. Surprisingly, these small dots are quantum dots with sizes ranging from 1 to 4 nm. With the increase of voltage, the proportion of large size quantum dots increases (Fig. S1). In order to further prove the existence of quantum dots, fluorescence spectra and 365 nm UV were used to detect the prepared samples (Fig. S2).

In order to investigate the effect of voltage on the quality of graphene, all samples prepared with an alternating frequency of 0.01 Hz were measured by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the intensity of D peak increases with the increase of voltage. When voltage changes from ±5 to ±10 V, ID/IG ratio of the samples increases from 0.69 to 1.13, indicating that the ordered structure is destroyed at high voltage. This is consistent with most of the literature reported [30,31]. When the voltage changes from ±5 to ±8 V, the 2D peak shifts to low wavenumber (Fig. 4b), which indicates that the prepared graphene sheets become thinner [32]. Graphene with large defect and thickness can be easily obtained at high voltage. Therefore, a voltage of 8 V is selected for other explorations.

Fig. 5 a presents the XRD spectra of graphite powders obtained from different alternating frequencies. It can be seen that the (002) peak of graphite at 26° gradually weakens in the frequency range of 0.1–0.01 Hz, which implies that graphite exhibits a decreased degree of graphitization. In order to observe the difference more clearly, XRD spectra of graphite powder prepared by 0.05 Hz, 0.03 Hz, 0.01 Hz and direct voltage are shown in Fig. 5b. With the decrease of alternating frequency, the peak value shifts to the left, which indicates that the low frequency is beneficial to increase the layer spacing. It is obvious that graphite prepared by direct voltage has a characteristic peak around 10° in Fig. 5b, which is attributed to the prolonged oxidation of the anode. The results of FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 5c) also show that the oxidation degree of graphene prepared by direct voltage is higher than that of graphene prepared by alternating voltage. Fig. 5d shows that when the alternating frequency changes from 0.01 Hz to 0.1 Hz, the value of ID/IG changes from 0.87 to 0.83. This indicates that the alternating frequency has little effect on the quality of graphene prepared. The quality of graphene prepared by alternating voltage is higher than that prepared by direct voltage (ID/IG = 1.2). The result is consistent with results of XRD and FT-IR. Fig. 6 are the TEM images of graphene prepared at different alternating frequencies. With the increase of frequency, the size of graphene lamellae also increases. Compared with high frequency, low frequency is good for sufficient cutting, which is helpful for the preparation of small size particles. At the same, the size distributions of the obtained samples at 0.01 Hz, 0.1 Hz and direct voltage are shown in Figs. S3 and S4. In order to calculate the size distribution, 100 sheets per sample were counted. The size of graphene prepared by direct voltage is mainly concentrated in 300–700 nm, while that prepared by alternating voltage is mainly concentrated in 100–400 nm. We can get that alternating voltage is advantageous to the preparation of graphene with low oxidation degree and small size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the distance between electrodes
Dispersibility of samples prepared by alternating voltage and direct voltage
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene prepared by alternating voltage (±8 V, 0.01 Hz) and direct voltage (8 V) were dispersed respectively by sonication in IPA, NMP, ethanol, and water solvents. Fig. S6 shows the photographs of graphene dispersions standing at different times. For graphene prepared by alternating voltage, it can be well dispersed in all solvents. After one month, no graphene precipitations are observed in all solvents. To explain the stability of graphene-GQDs in NMP, IPA, Water, and ethanol, zeta potentials of graphene-GQDs in all solutions were measured. Zeta potentials of graphene-GQDs in NMP, IPA, water, and ethanol are −35.3 mV, −44.1 mV, −42.9 mV, and −32.1 mV respectively. Their absolute values are all higher than 30 mV and so graphene-GQDs has good stability in these solutions [33]. On the contrary, it is clearly found that graphene prepared by direct voltage has sedimentation in IPA after standing for a week and in ethanol one month later. Paredes [34] has reported that graphene oxide has short-term stability in ethanol and IPA solution. This also proves that the oxidation degree of graphene prepared by direct voltage exfoliating is higher than that prepared by alternating voltage exfoliating.

From the above exploration, we can find that alternating voltage is more favorable to obtain graphene with low oxidation degree and small size than direct voltage. The specific surface area of graphite exfoliated by alternating voltage is slightly higher than that exfoliated by direct voltage (Fig. S7), which further proves that alternating voltage is more advantageous to prepare small size particles. To investigate the exfoliating mechanism, we have compared the exfoliation of alternating voltage with direct voltage in solution (Fig. S8). It can be clearly observed that exfoliation happens only at the anode under direct voltage and at two electrodes under alternating voltage. In the process of exfoliation, intercalation plays an important role. CV was performed in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolytes. In Fig. S9, there are two pairs of peaks. Reduction peaks at around 0.3 V and 0.5 V versus Ag/AgCl indicate the process of exfoliation and expansion. Both graphite electrodes appear bubbles at the voltage of 0.5 V during the reduction scan from 1.6 V to 1.0 V, which may be related to electrolysis of water [35]. The deintercalation of SO4 2− anions from graphite layer is about 0.3 V (vs Ag/AgCl), and an oxidation peak (corresponding to 0.4 V) reverse scan from 0 to 1.6 V indicates the intercalation of SO4 2− anions [12]. So we speculate the mechanism of exfoliation under alternating voltage depicted in Fig. 9 . At a voltage of 1.23 V, the water of the anode hydrolyzes into oxygen and ozone. However, the high overpotential (8 V) of the anode decomposes water into hydroxyl radicals (HO·) [36,37]. These hydroxyl radicals first oxidize the carbon on the edge of graphite to form oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups. The opening of graphite boundaries facilitates the insertion of SO4 2− and H2O. When the voltage changes from positive potential to negative potential, the intercalated SO4 2− and H+ will be reduced to SO2 and H2, respectively. This will cause the graphite to expand and fall off the graphite plates. At the same time, carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups on graphite are reduced during cathodic electrochemical reaction [14]. Complex electrochemical reactions also produce oxygen and carbon dioxide, which can also help to expand and exfoliate. Finally, the electrochemical treated graphite with expanded structure is obtained (Fig. S10). The oxidation and sufficient cutting promote the formation of GQDs (Fig. S11). The composite products of graphene and GQDs were obtained by ultrasonic treatment. The composition of graphene-GQDs composites was analyzed by XPS and the results are presented in Fig. S12. The C1s spectrum clearly indicates a certain degree of oxidation with three components corresponding to carbon atoms in different functional groups: CC (284.2 eV), CO (286.1 eV), and OCO, 289.3 eV). The O1s peak can be deconvoluted into two different components. The OH and CO are separately located at 530.1 eV and 533.3 eV. The existence of oxygen-containing functional groups from the GQDs contributes to the stable dispersion of graphene in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of DA at graphene-GQDs modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, sample prepared under the condition of ±8 V, 0.01 Hz and 2 cm was used to modify the GCE. The bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, graphene/GCE, graphene-GQDs/GCE (direct voltage) and graphene-GQDs/GCE (alternating voltage) were characterized by EIS and CV in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution (include 0.1 M KCl). As shown in Fig. 10 a, the graphene-GQDs/GCE (alternating voltage) shows a lower charge transfer resistance than other modified electrodes, indicating that graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage increases the electron transfer rate of the surface of proposed sensor. Fig. 10b displays the CV curves of modified electrodes. The background current of graphene-GQDs/GCE (alternating voltage) is larger than others, illustrating that graphene-GQDs prepared by alternating voltage enhances the electrochemical response of the graphene-GQDs/GCE.

The electrochemical response for DA on bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, graphene/GCE, graphene-GQDs/GCE (direct voltage) and graphene-GQDs/GCE (alternating voltage) was studied using CV. Fig. 11 a shows the CVs of different modified electrodes in 0.1 M PBS solution including 0.1 mM DA. The CV curves show a reversible peak, with the forward scan denoting the oxidation of dopamine to dopamine-o-quinone and the reverse scan denoting its corresponding reduction. The bare GCE shows small oxidation peak current (1.769 µA) and big peak potential separation (ΔEp = 156 mV). The peak current and ΔEp of graphene/GCE, GQDs/GCE, graphene-GQDs/GCE (direct voltage) and graphene-GQDs/GCE (alternating voltage) are 8.7 µA and 53 mV, 5.8 µA and 49 mV, 14.18 µA and 32 mV, 21.94 µA and 29 mV, respectively. Compared with other modified electrodes, the graphene-GQDs/GCE (alternating voltage) shows best electrochemical response, which may be due to the activity provided by GQDs and the high electrical conductivity provided by graphene.

The amount of electrocatalyst on the surface of modified electrode is a significant factor affecting its electrochemical response. The effects of the amount of coated graphene-GQDs on the electrode surface were studied in detail (Fig. S13). The oxidation peak current of DA gradually increases when the amount of coated graphene-GQDs gradually increases from 3 μL to 5 μL, while the peak current starts to decrease if the amount of graphene-GQDs is higher than 5 μL. It may be the thickness of the coated film increases, thereby increasing the electron transfer distances between the redox center and the electrode. As a consequence, 5 μL of graphene-GQDs suspension is used to modify the GCE in the following experiments.

Effects of pH on the electrochemical behaviours of DA at graphene-GQDs (alternating voltage) were studied in 0. 1 M PBS at pH range (5.6–8.8). As shown in Fig. 11b, the maximum oxidation peak current of DA is observed at pH 6.0. Therefore, pH 6.0 is used in the following experiments. Fig. 11c presents the relationship curves between pH and the peak potential for the cathode and anode. The equations for the peak potential with pH are Epa = −0.062 pH + 0.6107 (V, R2 = 0.995) for the anode, and Epc = −0.066 pH + 0.5738 (V, R2 = 0.984) for the cathode. It is found that the slopes for the cathode and the anode approach the theory value of 59.2 mV pH−1. This proves that the number of electrons is the same as the number of protons in the transfer process.

In order to assess electrochemical kinetics, CVs (Fig. 11d) were performed at different scan rate (10–100 mV s−1). The peak current (Ip) increases with increase in the scan rate (ν) and the anodic (Ipa = 10.7368 ν1/2 –18.7212, R2 = 0.997) and cathodic peak currents (Ipc = −8.6710 ν1/2 + 21.0658, R2 = 0.993) have a linear relationship with the ν1/2, indicating that the oxidation behavior of DA at graphene-GQDs is diffusion controlled process [38–41]. In addition, the peak potential of anode and cathode is linearly related to the logarithm of scan rate. The equations are Epa = 0.010 log ν + 0.2423 (R2 = 0.999) and Epc = 0.1753–0.00738 log ν (R2 = 0.998), respectively. According to Laviron's equations [42]:
(1)
E
pa
=
E
0 '
+
2.3 R T
( 1 - α ) n F lg ν
(2)
E
pc
=
E
0 '
-
2.3 R T
α n F
lg ν
The calculation shows that α (electron-transfer coefficient) is 0.57 and n (electron-transfer number) is 1.90.

Fig. 11e presents DPV curves of the sensor in pH 6.0 PBS containing various concentration of DA. As the concentration of DA increases, the oxidation current gradually increases and finally reaches the maximum. In the range of 0.1–100 μM, the fitted linear regression equation (Fig. 11f) is Ip = 1.0121 C + 3.9562 (R2 = 0.999), with the detection limits of 0.03 μM (S/N = 3). The sensitivity of graphene-GQDs is 14.25 µA µM−1 cm−2, which is obviously higher than that of the reported sensors in Table 1 .

Finally, the interference of inorganic ions on DA detection has been investigated. These ions (for Na+, K+, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−) are not electrically active, so their introduction can not cause any electrochemical response under the optimum conditions for detection of DA. In addition, their existence in the sample doesn't influence on the redox reaction of DA and bring the interference, because these ions cannot react with DA to form stable complex. The investigation indicates that the presence of 10 mM of KCl, NaCl, Na2CO3, and Na2SO4 hardly causes an interference on the peak current of DPV in Fig. S14a. In serum, AA, DA and UA survive together. So it is necessary to detect the effects of AA and UA on DA detection. In 100 µM DA, 1 mM AA or UA, AA and UA give a sensitive DPV response, but UV and AA do not affect the DPV signal of DA. And peak potentials of UA and AA on the DPV curves exist in an obvious different with that of DA [43]. Those results indicate that graphene-GQDs composites are good to detect DA in presence of the interfering agents. The sensing performance a single graphene-GQDs modified electrode was recorded continuously for 30 cycles and the results are shown in Fig. S14b. It can be seen that there is no change in their peak potential and peak current, suggesting that the higher sensing ability and excellent stability of the graphene-GQDs modified electrode.

The temporal stability of graphene-GQDs modified GCE toward the response of 100 µM DA was examined by CV at different intervals. The graphene-GQDs modified GCE lost only about 7.2% of the initial sensitivity of DA after 17 days, indicating that the appropriate storage stability of the sensor.

In this paper, a novel method of alternating electrochemistry combining ultrasonic treatment was used to exfoliate graphite. The results showed that the quality of the product from alternating voltage is better than that of direct voltage. The obtained product is the composite of graphene and GQDs, which can be dispersed in water stably. The existence of GQDs can hinder the agglomeration of graphene and facilitate the dispersion of graphene in water. Graphene-GQDs were used as an electrochemical sensor material, exhibiting good sensitivity, selectivity and stability toward DA.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21676023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2021.01.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Science and technology roadmap for graphene, related two-dimensional crystals, and hybrid systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.C. Ferrari, F. Bonaccorso, V. Fal'Ko, K.S. Novoselov, S. Roche, P. Bøggild, S. Borini, F.H. Koppens, V. Palermo, N. Pugno, Science and technology roadmap for graphene, related two-dimensional crystals, and hybrid systems, Nanoscale, 7 (2015) 4598-4810.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon monolayer phase condensation on Ni (111)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic confinement and coherence in patterned epitaxial graphene
